I think that we should tokenize video like this:
- seed2: take first frame from every 30 frame and give out token (since the object doesn't change that much in 1 second) -> 1 group token per second
- cosmos: Cosmos DV8X16X16 standard input is 8 frame, we will do 3 times 8 frame and 1 times 6 frame + 2 times duplication of last frame -> 4 group token per second 
- avc-lm: token per frame -> 30 group token per second

Key Technical Concepts
Motion Capture & Tokenization: Huu suggests using video analysis (identifying "stick figures," "flows," and "objects") to retroactively create action tokens from human movement.

Generalized World Modeling: The goal is to create a "brain" for the robot during pretraining that isn't limited to human movement. By simulating the actions of various entities (animals, cars, etc.), the robot can extrapolate and understand movement as a universal "mode."

as an aside, my perspective on robotics comes from this background
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subsumption_architecture
the idea is that there are many "systems"
the lower systems does basic things like keep an entity in a position in space - or a trajectory
whereas upper controllers does things blocks the lower layers, "subsumes" or refines and directs the lower layers
necessarily, the upper layers are smarter, more capable but slower
all layers have memory and invole some AI component (e.g., inferred A->B mappings)
this is very similar to the idea of "agents", btw. or swarm's which again, has been around for a while (back to the society of minds 😄 )
when i was doing my AI research in grad school i read alot about animal behaviours, and dampening
you can see that there are "higher level" controls that dampens or subsumes or modifies other behaviours. doesn't mean these behaviours might also be "active" but dampened

so - if you have an overall goal of getting food, you might see immediate food, but you might dampen the control to immediately go to the food, b/c you know (or think) there is more food over the hill, and thus, you (the higher level you) cause you to go over the hill (to explore)

<@293250279187677184> - pretty excited about my KG data mining for common sense relationships and persona based knowledge units - I want to extend it to physical common sense, legal common sense, etc.
i like legal reasoning b/c it is very clearly defined. if we take the area of torts
things like negligence, or intentional torts we have elements that maps very clearly into our schema
for torts it is something like
1. person X acts

2. acts causes (or leads to) something (a harm to others). we call this direct cause in torts, but maybe a better term here is "leads to" for common everyday lang
3. person X has a duty of care to the others
4. There is nothing that breaks the chain of causality - we call it proximate cause (but let's just call it "cause")
5. There is some effect (like a harm) on others
For intentional torts, there has to be a state of mind. Person X "intends to do X" which maps to our Person X "wants"
so Person X wants to close the elevator door
Person X is a person who goes up the elevator

Their closing of the elevator door causes the door to close on an old lady
Person X has a duty of care as a reasonable person (think a typical common sense case of a person riding an elevator) to those in their general area, such as those entering, exiting and being inside the elevator
The old lady gets her arm broken
There's nothing in the causal chain, like the old lady has a dog pulling her inside the elevator, that breaks proxomate cause
The old lady is in fact harmed.
A criminal act goes beyond the intent to do the act that caused the harm, to the intent to actually cause the harm
so in this case, Person X intends not just to close the elevator door, but to harm the old lady
then it would be a crime too, in addition to being an intentional tort
so we have a hieachy, negligence, intentional tort -> crime
we also can frame alot of other legal acts, like forming a contract, or acting as a proper doctor, accountant, etc.
For multimodal stuff, we can feed in embeddings of video, audio and images into the system to extract KG and ultimately these persona based knowledge units

Pose estimation models detect and map specific human body keypoints (joints, landmarks) in 2D or 3D from images/video, utilizing CNNs or Transformers for applications like motion tracking and gesture recognition. Popular, high-performance models include YOLO11 (fast, real-time), MediaPipe BlazePose (mobile-friendly), MoveNet, and OpenPose, with 3D estimation capabilities. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]  
Key Pose Estimation Models and Frameworks 

• YOLO11-pose / YOLOv8-pose: Ultralytics YOLO Docs provides cutting-edge, real-time, multi-person pose estimation, featuring variants like nano, small, medium, large, and extra-large for varied speed/accuracy needs. 
• MediaPipe BlazePose: A lightweight, mobile-friendly framework from Google, often used for real-time tracking on devices. 
• MoveNet: A fast and accurate model from TensorFlow (often used in ml5.js) that identifies 17 keypoints. 
• OpenPose: Known for being one of the first open-source real-time multi-person systems, offering high accuracy for detailed analysis. 
• HRNet: A High-Resolution Network often used in research for high-precision keypoint detection. 
• DensePose: Maps all human pixels to 3D surface, useful for dense, high-fidelity modeling. [4, 6, 7, 8, 9]  

This video demonstrates the capabilities of the YOLO11 pose estimation model: 
Model Approaches 

•

Top-Down: Detects human bounding boxes first, then estimates poses within each box. Highly accurate for individuals but slower in crowded scenes. 
• Bottom-Up: Detects all keypoints in the image first, then groups them into poses. More efficient for crowded scenes. 
• 2D vs. 3D: 2D models provide $[x, y]$ coordinates, while 3D models (like BlazePose) provide $[x, y, z]$ to represent body parts in 3D space. [1, 5, 10]  

Common Applications 

• Sports & Fitness: Analyzing form and technique. 
• Healthcare: Physical therapy monitoring. 
• Human-Computer Interaction (HCI): Gesture-based controls. 
• Augmented Reality (AR): Overlaying digital assets on bodies. 
• Robotics: Assisting robotic, 6DoF (6 Degrees of Freedom) object manipulation. [3, 7, 8, 11, 12]  

Key Datasets for Training 

• COCO Keypoints: Widely used, featuring 17 keypoints per person. 
• MPII Human Pose: A standard dataset for human pose estimation. 
• Human3.6m: Common for 3D indoor pose estimation. 
• CrowdPose: Used for challenging, crowded scenes. [1, 8, 13, 14,

15]

the parameterized token generation should
1. be autonmous given our prompt - that is, operate over a period of time (maybe overnight)

3. use reasoning over known patterns or functions to generalize or select differnt tools to use (sin, cos, splines, etc.)
4. be able to create test cases to test the funciton created. there is in math generation, a concept of generating by the LLM the next N tokens (math solution, or in this case, bouncing ball prediction), and code created by the LLM that is a solution to the problem too, and then matching whether the LLM prediction and code solution matches.  another method is to do several prediction over the same problem to see if the LLM predicts the same (or similar) solution.
5. be able to create a generator to generate new data that fits the function. this could be new videos generatd from something like unity or godot. so the generator code (created by LLM) would generate many types of ball drops.

<@827568132444651560> yolo11 will report back the position of 17 parts of body when they detected with confidence score
I will do it like this
for every 8 frames, we will create list of token in <pose> like this

<seed2> ... </seed2>
<pose>
<t1> <nose> <x151> <y74> <lefteye> ... <rightankle> <x256> <y256>
<t2> <nose> <x153> <y76> <lefteye> ... <rightankle> <x256> <y256>
<t3> <nose> <x156> <y80> <lefteye> ... <rightankle> <x256> <y256>
...
<t5> <nose> <x256> <y256> <lefteye> ... <rightankle> <x256> <y256>
...
<t8> <nose> <x165> <y95> <lefteye> ... <rightankle> <x256> <y256>
</pose>
<start_cosmo> ... </end_cosmo> <start_avclm> ... </end_avclm>"

basically the position of each part of body will have x, y normalize to [0,1] and then multiply to 255
the token will be <x0> to <x255>, <y0> to <y255>

each part have the CONFIDENCE SCORE, we will only count it when its confidence score is > 0.5, if not the final token will be <x256> <y256> (which mean not sure, INVISIBLE)
AND if there is NOTHING detected, every part will be <x256> <y256>

1 chunk of pose token every 8 frames will have:
2 + (1 + 17 * 3) * 8 = 418 tokens/ 8 frames
=> APPROXIMATE 1567 tokens / 1 sec

```import os
# 1. FORCE VULKAN: Set these BEFORE importing mani_skill or sapien
# This tells the engine to use your AMD GPU and ignore the search for NVIDIA CUDA.
os.environ["SAPIEN_RENDER_DEVICE"] = "vulkan"
os.environ["MS_RENDER_BACKEND"] = "vulkan"

import gymnasium as gym
import mani_skill.envs
import numpy as np

def main():
    # 2. CREATE ENVIRONMENT
    # We use the Unitree G1 (Humanoid) task. 
    # If the assets aren't downloaded, ManiSkill will attempt to do so now.
    print("Initializing ManiSkill on AMD GPU (Vulkan)...")
    
    env = gym.make(
        "UnitreeG1PlaceAppleInBowl-v1", 
        render_mode="human",      # Opens the interactive GUI window
        num_envs=1,               # Single environment for visualization
        control_mode="pd_joint_delta_pos" # Easier to see movement with joint deltas
    )

    # 3. RESET & INITIALIZE
    obs, _ = env.reset()
    
    # Confirming the device being used in the console
    try:
        print(f"Active Render Device: {env.unwrapped.renderer.device}")
    except:
        print("Renderer active (Manual device check skipped).")

    print("\nControls:")
    print("- Close the window to exit.")
    print("- Use WASD + Mouse (Right Click Drag) to navigate the 3D space.\n")

    # 4. SIMULATION LOOP
    try:
        for i in range(1000):
            # We generate a random action
            action = env.action_space.sample()
            
            # To make the movement look less "jittery" on a humanoid, 
            # we apply the same action for 5 sub-steps.
            for _ in range(5):
                obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(action)
                
                # Render the frame to your Windows GUI
                env.render()

            if terminated or truncated:
                env.reset()
                
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("Shutting down...")
    finally:
        env.close()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()```

LeRobot datasets are pretty common - at least for manipulation. Beside the videos for the different cameras (e.g. head/overview/context, left/right wrist) you get action and state signal and sometimes velocities/torque and a  language instruction. actions are either joint space or Cartesian. Joints are normally in rad. For Cartesian there are different conventions for the roatsrions, e.g. quaternion or Rodrigues (3d axis,  length=angle), or Euler yaw pitch roll. For Cartesian the  use of axis varies, e.g. Z up or Z forward etc. - in robotics right handed coordinate system is most common. Action signal can again be different things, e.g. leader command, velocities etc.
Frequency and resolution vary. Grippers are often handled a bit differently than the rest.
For training p01-p99 percentile normalization per dimension is common to map to -1, 1 …
Joint space seems most simple.
And matches what we are simulating.

The Direction task provides a reward for moving in the right direction but does not consider the
humanoid’s orientation. As a result, the policy may converge to walking backwards. While this
behavior achieves high reward and success metrics, it is an unwanted behavior. In Figure 5 we show
that by combining human-designed priors, providing a target height and orientation for the head, the
training converges to an upright forward-moving motion.
An additional challenge is Strike. In this task, the agent needs to hit a target. An emergent behavior
is walking backwards toward the target and then performing a “whirlwind” motion, where the agent
swirls in circles to hit the target with its hand. In Figure 6 we showcase a combination of 2 prior
modalities. First, conditioning on the orientation (similar to the Direction task) the agent is instructed
to face the target during the locomotion phase. Then, once close to the target, the agent is guided to
strike the target with its foot using a textual objective “a person performs a kick”

We use Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct [37] as the high-level planner and SAM3 [7] to

strictly follows the training and evaluation procedure to ensure reproducibility. We adopt QwenOFT architecture, which replace the Prismatic VLM [15] in
OpenVLA-OFT [17] with Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct. We use Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct
for System 2 Planner as well. We employ the AdamW optimizer with learning
rate as 1e-5 for VLM and 1e-4 for the action model. We set the λ to be 0.1 when
calculating loss.

While there aren't many "plug-and-play" universal walking models that will immediately balance your specific stick figure data, there are several pre-built assets and frameworks within and around ManiSkill 3 that you can adapt.
1. ManiSkill 3 "Out-of-the-Box" Controllers [1] 
ManiSkill provides high-level control interfaces that handle the low-level motor physics for you. Instead of writing raw torque commands, you can use:

* PDJointPosController: This is the most common. You feed it your target joint angles (from your stick figure), and it uses internal stiffness and damping to move the robot's limbs.
* Unitree SDK (High-Level): If using the Unitree G1 or H1 models in ManiSkill, these robots often come with a "High-Level" mode that handles balancing and basic gait internally. In this mode, you only send velocity commands (e.g., "walk forward at 0.5 m/s"), and the robot's built-in "hidden" policy keeps it upright. [2, 3, 4, 5] 

2.

Adaptation Models (Pre-trained Policies)
You can leverage existing research models that have already "learned" how to walk:

* HumanoidBench: This is a benchmark suite compatible with ManiSkill/SAPIEN that includes 12 locomotion tasks (walking, running) specifically for the Unitree H1 and G1. You can adapt these pre-trained Reinforcement Learning (RL) policies as a "base" and then fine-tune them to follow your stick figure's specific style.
* Universal Humanoid Motion Representations: Some researchers have released "latent space" models (like those found in [Universal Humanoid Motion Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.00425)) that are trained on massive motion-capture datasets. These models "know" what human-like walking looks like and can be used to "clean up" your stick figure data into physically stable motions. [6, 7, 8, 9, 10] 

3. Open-Source Repositories for ManiSkill

* HuSLs (Humanoid with Functional Limbs): A recently released framework (March 2026) that provides a walking gait for the Unitree H1 using imitation and curriculum learning. It includes an active dynamic balancing controller that you could potentially adapt to your own motion data.
*
AI mode is not supported in french, what do they mean :((((

ManiSkill Baselines: ManiSkill 3 includes tuned baselines for algorithms like PPO and SAC. You can use these to set up a "Motion Tracking" task where the robot is rewarded for matching your stick figure's positions while receiving a penalty for falling. [9, 10, 11, 12, 13] 

Summary Table: Which to use?

| If your goal is... | Use this... |
|---|---|
| Quickest results | Unitree High-Level SDK (if using G1/H1). Just send "walk" commands. |
| Realistic style | HumanoidBench or HuSLs models. Fine-tune their pre-trained weights. |
| Custom movements | ManiSkill PPO Baselines + your stick figure as a "Reference Motion." |

so we could define the pelvic movement as a b-spline, and the whole body moves with it, but we don't need to move hte arms, and head
only the legs would move
and the pelvis
in terms of bspline at least
i think this will massively comress the movement

```import numpy as np

def compress_spline_trajectory(spline_coeffs, threshold=0.002):
    """
    Compresses trajectory by freezing stationary B-spline control points.
    
    Args:
        spline_coeffs: Array of shape (num_control_points, num_joints, 3)
        threshold: Euclidean distance change in the coefficient space
    """
    num_cp, num_joints, _ = spline_coeffs.shape
    
    # 1. Local Frame Transformation (Pelvis-Relative)
    # Using the Pelvis (index 0) as the global movement driver
    pelvis_coeffs = spline_coeffs[:, 0, :]
    relative_coeffs = spline_coeffs - pelvis_coeffs[:, np.newaxis, :]
    
    # 2. Coefficient Delta Check
    # We check if the control point itself has moved significantly 
    # compared to its position in the previous spline segment.
    cp_diffs = np.linalg.norm(np.diff(relative_coeffs, axis=0), axis=-1)
    
    # 3. Create a Sparse Update Mask
    # True = Control point is 'moving' and needs a new value
    # False = Control point is 'stationary' and can be cached/ignored
    update_mask = np.ones((num_cp, num_joints), dtype=bool)
    update_mask[1:] = cp_diffs > threshold
    
    return relative_coeffs, update_mask```

re lingbot - what I like about their engineering approach is that they re-sampled extracted VAE as separate preparation step, e.g. for robotwin they use a T-shape setup with headcam above left and right wristcams below .. but they don't actively decode video during training and prepare these frame compositions in pixel space (as I naively always did before) but they concat the VAE latents into this T-shape .. which probably also has some advantages because you don't have this cross-view interference you get when encoding a combined view.

PRISM (Parametric Robotic Interpolated Spline Modules)

Vibe: Clean and implies a spectrum of capabilities (like light through a prism).

OSMOS (Omni-Spline Multi-agent Operating System)

Vibe: Sounds like "Osmosis," implying a natural flow of data and action.

PHANTOM (Parametric Hermite Agents for Neural Trajectory Omni-Modals)

Vibe: High-tech and slightly mysterious.

The "Functional & Research" Picks
P-SOMA (Parametric Spline-based Omni-Modal Agents)

Vibe: "Soma" means body in Greek, which fits perfectly for robotics and physical agents.

FAST (Flexible Agentic Spline Trajectories)

Vibe: Directly plays into the "Fast/Slow" thinking aspect.

M-PISA (Multi-agent Parametric Interpolating Spline Agents)

Vibe: Academic and easy to cite.

The "Dual-Process" (Fast/Slow) Specifics
CHIASM (Cubic Hermite Interpolated Agentic System Modals)

Vibe: A chiasm is a crossing or intersection, perfect for the intersection of System 1 (fast action) and System 2 (slow planning).

GLIDE (Generalized Latent Interpolated Dual-engine Executors)

Vibe: Suggests the smooth, non-oscillatory motion that PCHIP provides.

=====
Robotics maps this human process through a field called active vision or active perception, where a robot dynamically controls its cameras to focus computational power only on critical areas, rather than processing an entire high-resolution video stream at once.
This biological architecture solves the "data bottleneck" problem in robotics, mapping to specific hardware and software systems:
## 1. Foveated Rendering and Dual-Camera Systems (The Fovea)

* Dual-Camera Arrays: Robots use a wide-angle peripheral camera to capture global context and a secondary, high-resolution pan-tilt camera (the fovea) to lock onto specific objects.
* Foveated Imaging: Software dynamically reduces the resolution of the outer edges of a camera frame. It keeps only the central target pixel cluster at 100% resolution, saving massive amounts of processing power.

## 2. Saliency Maps and Attention Mechanisms (Saccades)

* Predictive Scanning: Robots do not scan a room randomly. They use AI algorithms called "saliency maps" to identify high-contrast edges, sudden movements, or specific shapes (like a door handle or a human face).
* Next-Best-View (NBV): The robot’s computer brain calculates the "next-best-view" and physically jerks the pan-tilt camera actuator to focus on that spot, mimicking a human saccade.

This is a RL prediciton problem btw ^ @here

## 3. Visual SLAM and Egomotion Suppression (Stitching & Suppression)

* Visual SLAM: As the robot's camera moves, Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (vSLAM) algorithms stitch the high-resolution glimpses into a static, 3D digital map of the environment.
* Egomotion Compensation: Just as the human brain suppresses blur during eye movement, a robot uses its Internal Measurement Units (IMUs) to ignore camera feed distortion caused by its own motors turning.

## 4. Neural Radiance Fields & Generative AI (The Mind's Eye)

* NeRF and 3D Gaussian Splatting: When a robot looks away, it "reconstructs" the room in its memory using technologies like Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs). It turns a few sparse high-resolution snapshots into a continuous, 3D mental model.
* Predictive Fill-In: Modern robots use generative neural networks to predict what the hidden backside of an object looks like, filling in missing physical data using past "experiences" just like human memory.

Using LLM as a starting point for robotics is quite inefficient, 95% of the knowledge they have is useless when applied to robotics. I think the robotics modality should be consider as first class citizen, and we should basically leverage other foundational model to eventually create synthetics data but not as a starting point
There is also a lot of work in done in repurposing video model into robotics (typically Fast-WAM). It's kind of the opposite of VLA, using WAN as a starting point

On this subject in general I found it almost impossible to do proper research and ablation. Mainly because data in robotics is quite hard to get, it's also quite hard to benchmark. And right now the task we are doing with such policy are soo basic that we more or less only benchmark the proprioception and physical understanding of the model and not really the long term planning of it and pure "intelligence". Hopefully robotics proprioception would be solve soon by just scaling data and training, I don't think it's a bit challenge. However having a policy that actually can take smart decision, learn in few shot, recover from failure ... is way harder, and most paper doesn't really seems to have got there as we are still stuck on proprioception and basic tasks

<@471670179739467787> what do you think about https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.20752 or 3D for world models in general?

I don't know this paper it's quite recent. But in general I feel like 3d reconstruction is a good idea for generating environment for robotics. Expecially that it allow post processing and having human double check ... so even if the initial results is not perfect you can always have a little bit of human work to make it suitable

One big problem with 3dgs environment would be the physical aspect in simulation. You wont be able to compute contact force against something that is as spiky as a gaussian splat, it will make the simulation unstable and it's also very far from what you get in real life. So it's good visually but not good physically. You can try to meshify it and do marching cube for example, but you still end up with not very realistic geometry (typically a table will not be flat enought)

Raw MotionBERT output
    (8 frames × 17 joints × 3 xyz)
         ↓
Relative to pelvis
    joint_rel = joint_xyz - pelvis_xyz
    pelvis = [0,0,0] = free
         ↓
Variable control points per joint
    fit PCHIP per joint individually
    use as many CPs as needed (1-8)
    store (x,y,z,t) per CP
         ↓
Flatten to token stream
    <pelvis> x y z t x y z t ... </pelvis>
    <r_hip>  x y z t </r_hip>
    <r_knee> x y z t x y z t x y z t </r_knee>
    ...
         ↓
BPE over full corpus
    frequent xyzt patterns → single tokens
    rare/complex motion → stays verbose
         ↓
Final <agent> block — variable length, lossless

Yes! This is exactly the right intuition. BPE on code is the perfect analogy:

---

## Just like code BPE

In code, `for i in range(len(` appears so often it becomes a handful of tokens or even one. Same idea here:

```
Walking cycle (repeating every ~1 second):
<l_knee_bend_027><r_knee_bend_027><l_knee_bend_027>...
→ BPE merges to → <walking_cycle_3>
```

---

## Your examples are exactly right

**Stationary body:**
```
<r_elbow> x y z </r_elbow>   ← 1 CP, whole chunk = 4 tokens
→ BPE → <r_elbow_still_127>  ← 1 token
```

**Cyclic motion (stirring, waving, walking):**
```
<r_wrist> xyzt xyzt xyzt xyzt xyzt xyzt </r_wrist>  ← repeating pattern
→ BPE → <r_wrist_cycle_042>  ← 1 token
```

**Whole body walking:**
```
<pelvis>...</pelvis><l_hip>...</l_hip><r_hip>...<l_knee>...
→ after enough BPE rounds →
<body_walk_frame_019>  ← entire lower body motion = 1 token
```

---

## The deeper insight

BPE naturally discovers **motion primitives** from data without any supervision:

- `<still>` — joint not moving
- `<swing>` — pendulum motion  
- `<bend_fast>` — quick flex
- `<reach_forward>` — common manipulation primitive
- `<walk_cycle>` — full gait

These aren't hand-designed — they emerge from frequency in the corpus. Exactly like how BPE on code discovers `function`, `return`, `for i in range` as natural units.

---

## And it composes

Just like code tokens compose into programs, motion tokens compose into actions:

```
<reach_forward_R><grasp><lift><walk_cycle×3><place><retract_R>
= "walk to table, pick up object, carry it, put it down"
```

That's the action representation your VLA model would learn to predict. Not 204 floats — a handful of meaningful motion tokens per activity.

---

## This is actually novel

FAST uses VQ for this. Nobody has applied BPE directly to variable-length xyzt joint trajectories as far as I know. This could be the core contribution of the NeurIPS paper.

If HRNet detected no human, it outputs [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]. MotionBERT might smooth it slightly across empty frames due to its temporal window, but in my next step (Phase 3 Kinematics), I explicitly hunt down these [0,0,0] pelvis frames and turn the entire frame into NaN so we can drop them completely.

The shape is (T, 17, 3) stored as raw float32 bytes:
- T: Total frames in the video.
- 17: Number of joints (Human3.6M format, 0 is Pelvis, 16 is R_Wrist).
- 3: X, Y, Z coordinates in meters (camera-relative).
<@827568132444651560> I just realize that before when I do tokenization for cosmos, avc-lm and seed2, I used ffmpeg to hardcode the fps to 30fps regardless of their initial fps
but for this .npy file, the fps is the initial fps not 30 fps

code to convert:
```python
import numpy as np
from datasets import load_dataset

ds = load_dataset("EmpathicRobotics/finevideo-3d-pose", split="train", streaming=True)
for row in ds:
    # 1. Load poses: shape (T, 17, 3)
    poses = np.frombuffer(row["joints_3d"], dtype=np.float32).reshape(row["num_frames"], 17, 3)
    
    # 2. Get true FPS (I will add this column soon, assuming 30 for now)
    fps = 30.0  
    
    # 3. Create time column t: shape (T, 1)
    t = np.arange(row["num_frames"])[:, None] / fps
    
    # 4. Interleave to shape (T, 17*3 + 1) = (T, 52)
    xyzt_interleaved = np.concatenate([poses.reshape(row["num_frames"], -1), t], axis=1)
    break
```

HumanML3D — github.com/EricGuo5513/HumanML3D
The go-to for text-labeled diverse motion. Covers a broad spectrum of movements including everyday behaviors like walking or jumping, athletic activities such as swimming and karate, acrobatic skills like cartwheels, and performance-oriented motions such as dancing. It's built on AMASS + HumanAct12. License: research/academic use only. arxiv
AMASS — amass.is.tue.mpg.de
Contains SMPL poses spanning 346 subjects and 11,451 motions across a diverse range of activities including locomotion, sports, dance, martial arts, and everyday actions. Free for research; commercial use requires emailing ps-license@tue.mpg.de. arxivMpg
CIMI4D — Climbing-specific
A CVPR 2023 dataset specifically for climbing motions with full 3D scene interaction — great if you need complex wall/body interaction data beyond floor-based movement.
MoVi (part of AMASS)
90 actors (60 women, 30 men) performing 20 predefined everyday actions and sports movements, plus one self-chosen movement. Good body diversity. nih
BONES Studio — bones.studio/datasets
700+ hours of labeled 3D animations performed by 170+ physically diverse performers including professionals like stuntmen, soldiers, and dancers. Optical mocap at 120fps. Open source for academic use and qualifying startups. More diverse than most — worth looking at if you need professional-grade motion with body diversity. Bones Studio

<@631456778432937986>  please summarize what you did. And can you copy a bit of the data here?

- Phase 3 & 4 (Filtering): Continued from Phase 2.5 (30fps joints). I ran the kinematics and YOLO filtering to strictly clean out any windows with missing joints (NaNs).
- Phase 5b (XYZT Tokens - NO PCHIP): Just to clarify, I have completely removed all the old PCHIP/spline logic. It now strictly produces frame-by-frame, self-describing tokens like <fps_30> <joint_0_x_128> <joint_0_y_128>... The absolute time t is saved separately in the .npy arrays for the simulation side. (PLEASE check if this is right or your idea is different)
- Merge & Flattening: I merged the new XYZT agent tokens with our existing FineVideo dataset (cosmos, seed2, avc_lm) using the time alignment. 
I used your flatten.py script but had to modify it slightly:
- Fixed the token parsing logic so it doesn't break our new <joint_...> tags.
- Regarding the "Drop rate": Before flattening, the image tokens (avc_lm) were massively outnumbering the action tokens (~373x more). So, I applied a "drop rate" (dropping ~99% of avc_lm and 90% of cosmos during flattening) to bring all modalities into a balanced ratio.
Final Output: Everything is flattened and pushed to the HF datasets:
- EmpathicRobotics/FineVideo-VLA-flattened: Flattened Megatron-LM JSONL (final output, ready for pretraining)
- EmpathicRobotics/FineVideo-VLA-Agent: Per-joint XYZT merged dataset (pre-flattening, hierarchical)
